bed5cb668d0b.title: Subnautica 2 publisher’s CEO used ChatGPT in failed bid to avoid paying US$250m bonus to own studio head, court hears
bed5cb668d0b.summary:
Court orders Krafton’s CEO to reinstate Unknown Worlds’ leadership after they were ousted using an AI-generated plan

A South Korean gaming publisher who hatched a plan using ChatGPT to remove the heads of one of its own game studios in a bid to avoid paying US$250m has been ordered by a US court to reverse the removal.

The dispute stems from South Korean game developer Krafton’s acquisition of Unknown Worlds Entertainment, makers of the Subnautica video game, for $500m in 2021.

2b5a9ba1f4cb.title: Instagram to remove end-to-end encryption for private messages in May
2b5a9ba1f4cb.summary:
Meta’s announcement comes after years of criticism from child safety groups over feature

Instagram will stop encrypting private messages between users from May, after enduring years of criticism from law enforcement and child safety groups over the feature.

Meta quietly announced this month on its help page for Instagram and in an updated 2022 news post that end-to-end encryption would no longer be available on direct messages between users on Instagram from 8 May 2026.

В результате, акции и облигации на Уолл-стрит снизились, поскольку это может замедлить планы по снижению процентных ставок. Настроение рынка также омрачено продолжающимся конфликтом в Иране.

2197c32cf65f.title: Butterflies crossing oceans, moths navigating by the stars: unravelling the mysteries of insect migrations
2197c32cf65f.summary: Научные исследования сейчас сосредоточены на изучении масштабных миграций насекомых, таких как бабочек и мотыльков, которые пересекают огромные расстояния.  Эти миграции, ранее практически незамеченные, становятся особенно важными для изучения в связи с резким сокращением численности насекомых.  Ученые используют современные методы для отслеживания их перемещений, опираясь на исторические наблюдения, например, за массовыми миграциями в Пиренеях в 1950 году.
2197c32cf65f.original_description:
Trillions of insects embark, largely unnoticed, on epic journeys every year across mountain ranges, deserts and seas, and it is only now, as their numbers suffer huge declines, that scientists are tracking their movements

On a cloudless sunny day in October 1950, ornithologists Elizabeth and David Lack stood on a mountain pass in the Pyrenees and observed a once-in-a-lifetime spectacle – clouds of migrating insects.

Up to 500 butterflies were fluttering past them

ce301d8a2827.title: Meteor over Ohio causes large boom heard as far away as Pennsylvania
ce301d8a2827.summary:
Nasa spokesperson says meteor was traveling at 45,000mph but no reports of debris found

A meteor over Ohio caused a large boom that jolted people as far away as Pennsylvania on Tuesday morning, Nasa has confirmed.

The meteor entered the atmosphere at about 9am local time on Tuesday, producing a sonic boom felt across a wide swath of northern Ohio and beyond. Reports poured in from Cleveland and other sectors as far east as Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, and into New York state.

2a4f6ee9647a.title: Is this the world’s first quantum battery? Australian scientists say so
2a4f6ee9647a.summary:
Researchers say their prototype is a big step towards fully functioning batteries with rapid charging times

Australian scientists have developed what they say is the world’s first proof-of-concept quantum battery.

Quantum batteries, first proposed as a theoretical concept in 2013, use the principles of quantum mechanics to store energy, and have the potential to be more efficient than conventional batteries.

5d6f3a6b8e1b.title: Ancient skeleton unearthed in France is latest to be found sitting upright
5d6f3a6b8e1b.summary:
Scientists trying to work out why Gauls chose to bury some of their dead in seated position facing west

Children at a primary school in eastern France found a strange attraction next to their playground this week: a skeleton sitting upright, peeking out of a circular pit.

It is the latest in a series of bodies discovered in the city of Dijon that were buried in a seated position facing west.

3c1ee60e83fa.title: How you walk reveals to others how you are feeling, researchers say
3c1ee60e83fa.summary:
Study highlights the movements in people’s gait that give away most about their emotional state

A long face is not the only sign that someone is down in the dumps. How people walk is revealing too, particularly the swing of the arms and legs, researchers say.

Scientists asked volunteers to guess people’s emotions from video clips of them walking and found that bigger swings portrayed more aggression while smaller swings implied fear and sadness.

b014c4da96c5.title: Desperate parents calling pharmacies for meningitis jab as stocks run low
b014c4da96c5.summary: В связи с вспышкой менингита в Кенте, родители в панике обращаются в аптеки за вакциной, но запасы быстро истощаются. Спрос резко возрос, что привело к дефициту вакцин и затруднениям аптек в их пополнении.
b014c4da96c5.original_description:
Supplies becoming sparse amid rise in demand since Kent outbreak, which has killed two and left 13 seriously ill

Worried parents are contacting pharmacies in an “increasingly desperate” effort to get their children vaccinated against meningitis after the outbreak in Kent that has killed two young people and left 13 seriously ill.

The surge in demand has led to stocks of the vaccine running so low that many pharmacies cannot get hold of supplies from wholesalers.

3bb742189268.summary: В статье The Guardian говорится о негативном влиянии политики Дональда Трампа на науку в США, в частности, о сокращении финансирования и увольнениях ученых.  Автор предлагает европейским странам воспользоваться ситуацией и привлечь талантливых специалистов, покидающих Соединенные Штаты, предоставив им привлекательные условия работы.

7ce63568d149.title: What’s behind the injectable peptide craze? – podcast
7ce63568d149.summary: В подкасте обсуждается растущая популярность нерегулируемых инъекционных пептидов, таких как BPC-157 и TB-500, среди сторонников биохакинга и оптимизации здоровья. Эксперты рассматривают, как эти вещества стали широко известны и какое влияние они могут оказывать на организм человека.

2ef599a73423.title: Exercise and brain function, hedgehog hearing, and can AI change our minds? – podcast
2ef599a73423.summary: В новом подкасте The Guardian обсуждаются три интересных научных исследования: связь физических упражнений с улучшением работы мозга, способность ежей воспринимать высокочастотный ультразвук и влияние предвзятых алгоритмов искусственного интеллекта на формирование убеждений пользователей.  Рассматриваются потенциальные последствия этих открытий для здоровья человека, сохранения природы и влияния технологий на мышление.
2ef599a73423.original_description: The Guardian’s science editor, Ian Sample, talks to Madeleine Finlay about three eye-catching science stories from the week, including a study that explores the link between exercise and brain health. Also on the agenda: the discovery that hedgehogs can hear high-frequency ultrasound and what this could mean for their conservation, and new research examining how biased AI autocomplete tools can influence the beliefs of users.

18e0348ce90a.title: Was Iran really building a nuclear weapon? – podcast
18e0348ce90a.summary: В подкасте обсуждается вопрос о реальной угрозе ядерного оружия со стороны Ирана, которую часто использовал Дональд Трамп как оправдание для военных действий. ace9201131c0.title: Your daily coffee may be protecting your brain, 43-year study finds
ace9201131c0.summary: Your morning coffee or tea could be quietly supporting your brain health. A long-term study found that moderate consumption of caffeinated coffee or tea was linked to an 18% lower risk of dementia and better cognitive performance over time. The benefits appeared strongest at 2–3 cups of coffee or 1–2 cups of tea daily—and even held true for people genetically predisposed to dementia.

0c1ba50f1fc7.title: These strange pink rocks just revealed a hidden giant beneath Antarctica
0c1ba50f1fc7.summary: Обнаружение странных розовых гранитных валунов на горе Хадсон в Антарктиде позволило ученым сделать удивительное открытие: под ледником Пайн-Айленд скрывается огромная массивная порода размером почти 100 км в ширину и 7 км в толщину. Датировка пород периодом юрского времени и сопоставление с гравитационными данными разрешили разгадать загадку их происхождения, которая оставалась нерешенной десятилетиями.
0c1ba50f1fc7.original_description: Pink granite boulders sitting mysteriously atop Antarctica’s Hudson Mountains have led scientists to a stunning discovery: a hidden granite mass buried beneath Pine Island Glacier, stretching nearly 100 km wide and 7 km thick. By dating the rocks to the Jurassic period and matching them with gravity signals detected from aircraft, researchers solved a decades-old puzzle about their origin.

10bf504e6a6c.title: These dinosaurs had wings but couldn’t fly
10bf504e6a6c.summary: Some feathered dinosaurs may have briefly taken to the skies—only to give it up later. By studying rare fossils with preserved feathers, researchers uncovered a surprising clue hidden in molting patterns, revealing that Anchiornis likely couldn’t fly at all. Instead of the neat, symmetrical feather replacement seen in flying birds, these dinosaurs showed a messy, irregular molt—something only flightless animals exhibit.

45c0657070ff.title: AI uses as much energy as Iceland but scientists aren’t worried
45c0657070ff.summary: Использование искусственного интеллекта растет и потребляет огромное количество энергии, сопоставимое с потреблением целой страны, например, Исландии. Однако, по мнению ученых, глобальное влияние ИИ на климат может оказаться не таким значительным, как опасались, поскольку основная нагрузка приходится на локальные центры обработки данных. При этом, ИИ может стать важным инструментом для разработки экологически чистых технологий.
45c0657070ff.original_description: AI’s growing energy use sounds alarming, but its global climate impact may be far smaller than expected. Researchers found that while AI consumes huge amounts of electricity, it barely moves the needle on overall emissions. The real impact is more localized, especially around data centers. Meanwhile, AI could become a powerful tool for building greener technologies.

57faf3f233d4.title: JWST reveals a strange sulfur world unlike any planet we know
57faf3f233d4.summary: Astronomers have identified a strange new kind of exoplanet that challenges how scientists classify worlds beyond our Solar System. The planet, L 98-59 d, appears to contain a vast ocean of molten rock beneath its surface that traps large amounts of sulfur deep inside. Observations from the James Webb Space Telescope revealed unusual sulfur-rich gases in its atmosphere and a surprisingly low density for its size.

Observations from the James Webb Space Telescope revealed unusual sulfur-rich gases in its atmosphere and a surprisingly low density for its size.

dcc0f8a8c259.title: AI-powered robot learns how to harvest tomatoes more efficiently
dcc0f8a8c259.summary: Новый робот, использующий искусственный интеллект, научился более эффективно собирать помидоры. Вместо простого определения спелости, робот прогнозирует сложность сбора каждого плода и адаптирует свои действия, что привело к повышению успешности сбора до 81%. Данный прорыв может способствовать внедрению совместной работы роботов и людей на фермах.
dcc0f8a8c259.original_description: A new tomato-picking robot is learning to think before it acts. Instead of simply identifying ripe fruit, it predicts how easy each tomato will be to harvest and adjusts its approach accordingly. This smarter strategy boosted success rates to 81%, with the robot even switching angles when needed. The breakthrough could pave the way for farms where robots and humans work side by side.

bb5a83a36bb5.title: MIT scientists finally see hidden quantum “jiggling” inside superconductors
bb5a83a36bb5.summary: Ученые из MIT разработали новый микроскоп, использующий терагерцовый свет, позволяющий наблюдать скрытые квантовые движения внутри сверхпроводников. Это позволило впервые зафиксировать скоординированное движение электронов в волнообразном, не встречающем сопротивления состоянии. Открытие открывает новые перспективы в понимании работы сверхпроводников и может способствовать развитию высокоскоростных беспроводных коммуникаций.
bb5a83a36bb5.original_description: MIT physicists have built a powerful new microscope that uses terahertz light to uncover hidden quantum motions inside superconductors. By compressing this normally unwieldy light into a tiny region, they were able to observe electrons moving together in a frictionless, wave-like state for the first time. This discovery opens a new window into how superconductors really work. It could also help drive future breakthroughs in high-speed wireless communication.

1c846bf52a8c.title: He survived 48 hours without lungs and lived
1c846bf52a8c.summary: A critically ill 33-year-old man survived an almost unimaginable scenario—living for 48 hours without lungs—thanks to a groundbreaking surgical approach. After a severe flu-triggered infection destroyed his lungs and caused multiple organ failure, doctors removed both lungs entirely to stop the spread of infection. In their place, they used a specially engineered “artificial lung” system to oxygenate his blood and keep his body functioning while he stabilized.

24f18ca9e2a5.title: Scientists used 7,000 GPUs to simulate a tiny quantum chip in extreme detail
24f18ca9e2a5.summary: Researchers have pushed quantum chip design into a new era by simulating every physical detail before fabrication. Using a supercomputer with nearly 7,000 GPUs, they modeled how signals travel and interact inside an ultra-tiny chip. Unlike earlier “black box” approaches, this method captures real materials, layouts, and qubit behavior. The result is a powerful new way to spot problems early and build better quantum hardware faster.

9be006c3155a.title: Scientists finally reveal how this Alzheimer’s drug really works
9be006c3155a.summary: A key Alzheimer’s drug has finally revealed its secret. Researchers discovered that lecanemab works by activating the brain’s immune cells—but only through a specific part of the antibody called the Fc fragment. This piece acts like a trigger, prompting microglia to clear harmful amyloid plaques. The finding could reshape how future Alzheimer’s therapies are designed.

95cc4d187586.title: Study finds ChatGPT gets science wrong more often than you think
95cc4d187586.summary: A new study put ChatGPT to the test by asking it to judge whether hundreds of scientific hypotheses were true or false—and the results were far from reassuring. While the AI got it right about 80% of the time on the surface, its performance dropped significantly when accounting for random guessing, revealing only modest reasoning ability. Even more concerning, it frequently contradicted itself when asked the exact same question multiple times, sometimes flipping answers back and forth.

aba49cf2faca.title: Even JWST can’t see through this planet’s massive haze
aba49cf2faca.summary: Kepler-51d is a giant, ultra-light “super-puff” planet wrapped in an unusually thick haze that’s blocking scientists from seeing what it’s made of. Observations from JWST revealed that this haze may be one of the largest ever detected, possibly stretching as wide as Earth itself. The planet’s low density and close orbit don’t match existing models of how gas giants form or survive. Now, researchers are left with more questions than answers about how such a strange world came to be.

3bf794e2eb5c.title: Scientists link childhood stress to lifelong digestive issues
3bf794e2eb5c.summary: Early life stress may set the stage for long-term digestive problems by disrupting the gut-brain connection. Studies in both mice and thousands of children found links to symptoms like pain, constipation, and IBS. Scientists discovered that different biological pathways control different gut issues, hinting at more personalized treatments in the future. The research also highlights how a child’s early environment can have lasting physical effects—not just emotional ones.

50784d2a8b90.title: This massive crater could expose the heart of a lost planet
50784d2a8b90.summary: A mysterious metal-rich asteroid called Psyche has been baffling scientists for over two centuries, and its true origin remains one of the biggest unanswered questions in planetary science. 96101badfe3b.title: Scientists just discovered bull sharks have friends
96101badfe3b.summary: Bull sharks may have a reputation as lone hunters, but new research reveals they actually form social bonds and even have preferred “friends.” After six years of observing 184 sharks in Fiji, scientists discovered these animals don’t just mix randomly—they choose companions, swim together, and even follow one another in coordinated ways.

67b9889de9dc.title: NASA’s Webb captures a bizarre brain-shaped nebula around a dying star
67b9889de9dc.summary: The James Webb Space Telescope has revealed new details in a bizarre nebula that looks like a brain floating in space. Formed by a dying star, the “Exposed Cranium” nebula shows layered gas and a dark central divide that creates its eerie shape. Webb’s infrared view suggests powerful jets may be shaping the structure. The images capture a brief and dramatic phase in a star’s final evolution.

8de38cb6feaf.title: DNA origami vaccines could be the next leap beyond mRNA
8de38cb6feaf.summary: mRNA vaccines saved millions of lives during COVID-19 but have limitations like waning immunity and complex production. Scientists are now testing a new platform called DoriVac, which uses folded DNA nanostructures to better control how the immune system responds. In early studies, it produced strong antibody and T cell responses in both mice and human models. Researchers say it could lead to more stable, easier-to-manufacture vaccines for diseases like COVID-19, HIV, and Ebola.

d2ee8b868ec6.title: ADHD brains show sleep-like activity even while awake
d2ee8b868ec6.summary: Researchers have identified a surprising brain pattern that may help explain why people with ADHD often struggle to stay focused. Even while awake, their brains can slip into brief episodes of “sleep-like” activity during demanding tasks. These moments are linked to more mistakes, slower reaction times, and lapses in attention.

99d9babae40d.title: Fixing a tooth infection may improve blood sugar and heart health
99d9babae40d.summary: A new clinical study suggests that successful root canal treatment may benefit more than just dental health. Patients treated for infected teeth showed improved blood sugar control, healthier cholesterol levels, and reduced inflammation over time. The infection can allow bacteria to enter the bloodstream, potentially affecting metabolism and heart health. Clearing the infection appears to help reverse some of these harmful effects.

a9515806280f.title: The smell of Egyptian mummies is revealing 2,000-year-old secrets
a9515806280f.summary: The distinctive smell of ancient mummies is helping scientists decode the secrets of Egyptian mummification. By analyzing tiny traces of chemicals in the air around mummy samples, researchers identified dozens of compounds linked to oils, resins, beeswax, and bitumen used during embalming. The chemical clues reveal that mummification grew increasingly complex over thousands of years.

It’s not Greenland; it’s the Canadian Arctic.

abdc24c253d3.title: ‘Prince laughed like a kid as I painted “Free” on his stomach’: Steve Parke’s best photograph
abdc24c253d3.summary:
‘The art director wanted it to read “1999”. But Prince wasn’t having that. So the next thing I knew, I was writing “Free” on him with my fingers in cold paint’

Награду вручил генеральный директор украинских железных дорог в знак признательности за визит актера в страну и его участие в освещении событий. Пенн пропустил церемонию вручения премии "Оскар", чтобы поехать в Украину.
549225065d05.original_description:
The actor skipped Academy Awards ceremony to travel to Ukraine, where he was presented with alternative prize

Sean Penn has been presented with an Oscar fashioned from the metal of a Ukrainian railway carriage damaged by Russian missiles.

The statue, which is flat, silver and shaped like an Academy Award, was given to him by Oleksandr Pertsovskyi, CEO of Ukrainian railways, who told him: “You’re missing Oscars, so we made this one.”
